db_connection: CODE-ONLY — db_connection_results empty in extraction. Complexity scoring below is based on code structure (entity count, cross-DB references, owned types) — no live schema diff was possible.
rationale: Small entity surface (7 EF entities in CatalogContext + standard ASP.NET Identity schema), but two separate databases with an unenforced cross-database FK (BuyerId) increase migration risk — any migration tooling that assumes a single schema/transaction boundary will not be able to enforce referential integrity across CatalogDb and IdentityDb.

per_table_complexity.reason: Simple lookup table, 2 columns, no FK dependents besides Catalog.
Referenced by BasketItems and OrderItems; FK delete-behavior unknown (UNKNOWN, see data-quality-report.md). Migrating this table requires checking for orphaned-row handling.
Cross-database soft FK (BuyerId -> IdentityDb.AspNetUsers.Id) cannot be validated by migration tooling automatically.
Straightforward child table with two FKs into CatalogDb only.
Owned-type Address flattened into columns (5 extra columns) + cross-database soft FK BuyerId.
Owned-type CatalogItemOrdered flattened into columns; denormalized snapshot data needs careful handling during schema migration (don't accidentally re-link to live Catalog).
Standard ASP.NET Core Identity schema — well-documented, tooling (dotnet ef) handles migrations automatically. INFERRED, confidence 0.7.

cross_database_concerns.impact: Any migration that changes AspNetUsers.Id type/format (e.g. switching from string GUID to int) would silently break Basket/Order ownership with no DB-level error.

resolved_items.item: Buyer / PaymentMethod entities not present in supplied ef_entities list
resolved_items.resolution: CONFIRMED dead/aspirational code — not mapped to any DbContext (CatalogContext has only 7 DbSets; AppIdentityDbContext.OnModelCreating only calls base). No migration impact.

estimated_effort.schema_migration: LOW-MEDIUM — 9 confirmed tables (7 CatalogDb + ~6-7 Identity tables, standard scaffolding). NOTE: schema is already on PostgreSQL (Npgsql) per InitialPostgres migrations dated 2026-05-26 — see RC-001 in schema-catalogue.json — so a SQL-Server-to-Postgres migration may already be largely complete; remaining risk is the stale SQL Server connection strings still present in appsettings.Docker.json.
estimated_effort.data_migration: UNKNOWN — row counts not available (db not connected)
estimated_effort.application_code_changes: MEDIUM — repository/specification pattern (IRepository, IReadRepository) centralizes data access, which helps; but cross-DB BuyerId logic is spread across BasketService and OrderService (src/ApplicationCore/Services/).

change_records.what: Moved from unresolved_items to resolved_items: Buyer/PaymentMethod confirmed not mapped to any table, so they carry zero migration impact.
change_records.evidence_detail: src/Infrastructure/Data/CatalogContext.cs (7 DbSets only); src/Infrastructure/Identity/AppIdentityDbContext.cs (no custom entity config)

change_records.what: Added note that the codebase already has PostgreSQL migrations (InitialPostgres, 2026-05-26) registered via Npgsql, so a prior SQL Server -> Postgres migration appears largely done; the only loose end is stale SQL Server connection strings in appsettings.Docker.json.
